🟡 ReVillage Green Valley

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

ReVillage Green Valley is a place-rooted initiative reweaving community through land-based regeneration and collective care, responding to wildfires, housing displacement, ecological degradation, and social isolation.

Bioregional Context

Green Valley exists within West Sonoma County, California, centered around the town of Graton with approximately 1,800 residents. The bioregion is defined by rolling hills, vineyards, farmland, and forests, with the Green Valley watershed serving as a natural boundary.

Place
West County Museum, 261 S Main St, Sebastopol, CA 95472, United States
links
https://youtu.be/nowoYmBRijw

Tags

🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 💠 Collective_Ownership_Models ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🎒 Learning_Programs 🍥 Governance_Design 📣 Political_Advocacy

Background


The Green Valley watershed is a compact 40-square-mile bioregion in western Sonoma County, California, centered on Graton (about 1,800 residents). Rolling hills, vineyards, farmland, and forests define the area, with the watershed itself as a natural frame. Over the past century the local economy moved from apples toward wine; the community is now writing another chapter.

Origins and Network


ReVillage Green Valley grew out of grassroots organizing. They describe themselves as accidental bioregional organizers—the work began with love of place and gathering alongside neighbors who had long stewarded the land.

They envision a local economy grounded in ecology, equity, and cultural renewal, co-created with First Nations peoples, Latino farmworkers, Zen communities, and other long-term residents. Wildfires, housing pressure, ecological decline, and isolation are the overlapping crises; their response is to reweave community through land-based regeneration and shared care.

Activities

🔸

Green Valley Commons

 🔸

Arts & Museum of the Future

 🔸

Community Land Trust Formation

 🔸

ReVillage Co-Op Enterprises

 🔸

Local Governance Innovation

 🔸

ReVillaging Toolkit and Learning Exchange

Vision and Legacy


They are watching how commons, shared culture, and a regenerative economy might braid over time, and welcome partners who want to prototype village-scale regeneration built for interdependence, cultural vitality, and ecological repair.

By strengthening intergenerational, locally governed systems, they aim to offer a replicable model of “revillaging” for rural and peri-urban landscapes. The same crises—fire, displacement, ecological harm, isolation—are addressed by reweaving community through land-based regeneration and collective care.

In the Green Valley watershed—hills, vineyards, farmland, forest—people are practicing interdependence with one another, with nonhuman nature, and with the living Earth: one gas station, one firehouse, one enterprise at a time, starting from love of place and neighbors who show up to care.
